text: The surgeon performs a right hemicolectomy, beginning with the mobilization of the terminal ileum by dissecting the peritoneum. A medial approach is used for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy, visualizing the surgical trunk on the superior mesenteric vein. The iliocolic vein and right colic vein are divided, followed by a functional end-to-end anastomosis through an upper median small incision.
The surgeon begins the cholecystectomy by double-clipping and dividing the cystic duct, ensuring secure closure before proceeding with further dissection.

The surgeon uses intraoperative sonographic examination with Sonazoid contrast to visualize two liver metastases located in segment five, confirming their exact location and relationship to major blood vessels.

The surgeon performs pure laparoscopic segment V subsegmentectomy, beginning with liver parenchymal dissection after mobilizing the right side of the liver. Using a laparoscopic harmonic scalpel, the country line is opened to locate the right anterior glistoneal pedicle, which is double-clipped and divided using a tissue sealing device.

The surgeon continues the parenchymal dissection of the liver using a KUSA device, visualizing additional branches of the glistoneal pedicle in segment five. These branches are clipped and divided using the Enseal tissue sealing device.

The surgeon continues parenchymal dissection along the demarcation line using a harmonic scalpel. A peripheral branch of the middle hepatic vein from segment five is identified and clipped using Enseal before resection. Segment five is resected, containing both metastases with a safe surgical margin.

The surgeon places the resected segment five of the liver into a sterile bag for retrieval through a five-centimeter median incision, which was made for the anastomosis during the colectomy. This is part of a simultaneous laparoscopic resection procedure addressing colorectal cancer and synchronous liver metastasis.
